column4: We never realize the impact on our life choices until they lead us to a future we did not imagine.
When we pass away, what will happen to our memories, do they just disappear, or do they still exist somehow. What if -- what if we tried to save memories like a file on a computer?
What does memory mean to you?
Classical art is dying. I try to revive the art of music by incorporating it with code.

I was curious how people in the future are going to gain happiness and if the form of happiness will remain the same as how humans gain it right now. I thought a photo book can best illustrate that, and I tried my best to make it interactive by adding doodling and sound elements.

2030 is focused on show explicitly the reality of someone experiencing enslavement, following the journey of black people stolen from Africa and brought to Brazil to work on a system of slavery. The project aims to immerse users on what type of conditions and situations black people lived.

“Residual Signal” is an interactive internet art piece where a thousand years from now, machines living entirely in cyberspace accidentally detect a human presence through a webcam signal. They try to reconstruct what a human looks like using only 0s and 1s. This prototype focuses on the core mechanic: turning the live webcam feed into a face made entirely of binary characters, green on black, with periodic glitch effects that represent the machine’s unstable reconstruction from corrupted data.
I assume people will get more and more restless as the technology develops. In a world like that, we can only rely on each other to clear up all the uneasiness.

Step into a digital garden that blooms with your voice and fades with your silence, where every gesture weaves connections and every pause triggers decay. This interactive time capsule asks a question for the future: what do we truly value enough to preserve?
Play with a friend or even a stranger, and try to connect over two circles connecting!
An interactive CAPTCHA experience that is unusual. As you try to prove you’re human, the system begins to question why you continue.
With this project i wanted to find a different way of preserving my own cultural memory.
Same Side is an interactive piece about what gets lost when a language slowly changes — not the words, but the feelings they once carried.

An online robot toy that turns your face into a pixelated head and lets you drag, press and play with it like a childhood object. It imagines a future where physical toys are
remembered digital.
This is a city in endless extension. Click your mouse and move your hand, you can encounter new interactions.
